questions.questionText: Croup (acute laryngotracheobronchitis) is most commonly caused by:
questions.optionB: Parainfluenza virus (types 1 and 3 — steeple sign on AP neck X-ray, barking cough, inspiratory stridor)
questions.explanation: Croup: viral laryngotracheobronchitis. Commonest cause: parainfluenza virus (types 1, 3 most common). Peak: 6 months - 3 years. Features: barking/seal-like cough, inspiratory stridor, hoarse voice, low-grade fever. Symptoms worse at night. XR: steeple sign (subglottic narrowing on AP view). Treatment: mild → cool mist, single dose dexamethasone (0.6 mg/kg). Moderate-severe → nebulized epinephrine (racemic/L-epinephrine) + dexamethasone. Epiglottitis (H. influenzae type B): distinguished by drooling, toxic-appearing, thumb sign on lateral neck XR — medical emergency. Reference: OP Ghai — Croup.

questions.questionText: Bronchiolitis in infants is most commonly caused by:
questions.optionB: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV — peak 2-6 months, wheezing, hyperinflation on CXR)
questions.explanation: Bronchiolitis: lower respiratory tract infection in infants (<2 years, peak 2-6 months). Commonest cause: RSV (60-80%). Features: rhinorrhea → cough → wheezing → tachypnea → respiratory distress. CXR: hyperinflation, peribronchial thickening, atelectasis. Treatment: primarily SUPPORTIVE (oxygen, hydration, nasal suctioning). NOT helpful: bronchodilators (minimal benefit), antibiotics, steroids. Palivizumab: anti-RSV monoclonal antibody prophylaxis for high-risk infants (preterm, CHD, BPD). Reference: OP Ghai — Bronchiolitis.
